_hydrated.note: All calculated and aggregation fields are DB-computed values from vw_* views. Raw fields match the rulebook SSoT. Real study data lives in effortless-postgres/05b-customize-data.sql.

data.Name: loop-01
data.Title: Core domain: Study, Treatment, Stratum, CaseCell → SuccessRate falls out
data.NewConcept: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells
data.DomainQuestion: For each (stratum, treatment) cell in a study, what is the success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney stone study (Charig 1986): 2 strata (small/large), 2 treatments (A/B), 4 cells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment, what is its pooled (across all strata) success rate? That requires TreatmentSummary — aggregating CaseCells by treatment, ignoring stratum.

data.Name: loop-02
data.Title: TreatmentSummary: pooled rate per treatment across all strata
data.NewConcept: TreatmentSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: If you ignore stratum entirely, what is each treatment's overall success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled: A=273/350=78%, B=289/350=83%. B looks better — this is the misleading aggregate view.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each (stratum, treatment) pair, what is the success rate WITHIN that stratum? That requires StratumSummary — the view that contradicts the pooled view.

data.Name: loop-03
data.Title: StratumSummary: per-stratum rate per treatment — the true picture
data.NewConcept: StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: Within each stratum, which treatment has the higher success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Small: A=93.1% > B=86.7%. Large: A=73.0% > B=68.8%. A wins in BOTH strata. Contradiction with pooled view is now visible.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment pair in a study, does the pooled winner match the per-stratum winner? That requires TreatmentRanking — the node where the paradox either exists or doesn't.

data.Name: loop-04
data.Title: TreatmentRanking: does the pooled winner = the per-stratum winner?
data.NewConcept: TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: When pooled and stratified conclusions disagree — which treatment should actually be preferred?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled winner = B. Per-stratum winner = A in every stratum. Simpson's Paradox: IsReversal = true. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: WHY does the paradox occur? The stratifying variable (stone size) affects both treatment assignment AND outcome. Name it as a StratumVariable with a CausalRole field.

data.Name: loop-05
data.Title: StratumVariable: name the confounding factor, its causal role, and derive IsConfounder + IsParadoxExplained
data.NewConcept: StratumVariables (one per study); CausalRole field; IsConfounder boolean; IsParadoxExplained on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: What IS the stratum variable, why does it confound, and does the model witness its own explanation?
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: VariableName=stone_size, CausalRole=confounder, AffectsTreatmentAssignment=true, AffectsOutcome=true → IsConfounder=true → IsParadoxExplained=true on TreatmentRankings.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how large is the allocation imbalance? Derive TreatmentExposureFraction on CaseCells — the fraction of each treatment's cases that land in each stratum. High imbalance = confounding mechanism as a witnessed number.

data.Name: loop-06
data.Title: TreatmentExposureFraction: the allocation imbalance as a witnessed number
data.NewConcept: TreatmentExposureFraction field on CaseCells (Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment in this study)
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of each treatment's total cases fall in each stratum? High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding.
data.MockDataNote: A: 263/350=75% in large-stone stratum. B: 80/350=23% in large-stone stratum. The 52-point gap IS the mechanism — now a first-class derived number.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a degenerate study where treatment allocation is perfectly balanced across strata. IsReversal must be FALSE — not because the data says so, but because the algebra guarantees it. The model witnesses its own structural impossibility.

data.Name: loop-07
data.Title: Degenerate study: balanced allocation → IsReversal structurally impossible
data.NewConcept: Second Studies row (balanced-synthetic); equal TreatmentExposureFraction across strata; IsReversal = FALSE by construction
data.DomainQuestion: If every stratum gets the same treatment-allocation fraction, can a reversal occur? The DAG should witness that it cannot.
data.MockDataNote: Synthetic: A and B each assigned 50/50 to small/large strata. A=60% success both strata, B=50% both. Pooled: A=60%, B=50%. No reversal. IsParadoxExplained vacuously true (IsConfounder=false, no confounding, no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: Add Berkeley 1973 — same DAG, contested domain. Aggregate: women admitted at lower rate. Per-department: women admitted at equal or higher rate in most. IsReversal=true. But IsConfounder is ambiguous — department choice may be downstream of gender (mediator, not confounder). The model witnesses its own explanatory limits.

data.Name: loop-08
data.Title: Berkeley 1973: contested confounder — model reveals a PARTIAL paradox, not a clean reversal
data.NewConcept: Third Studies row (berkeley-1973); StratumVariable=department; CausalRole=contested; IsConfounder=false; IsParadoxExplained=false; PerStratumWinner=none (mixed, not unanimous)
data.DomainQuestion: Does Berkeley show a clean reversal? No — PerStratumWinner='none' because the per-stratum winner is mixed (female wins 3/4 depts, male wins 1). The model surfaces a structural limit of the reversal definition: partial paradoxes are real but don't satisfy the strict IsReversal criterion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed: male pooled 52.8% vs female 39.9% (large gap). Per dept: female wins A (82%>62%), B (68%>63%), D (35%>33%); male wins C (37%>34%). No unanimous per-stratum winner → IsReversal=FALSE by strict definition. The intuitive paradox is real; the formal paradox is partial.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ParadoxStrength (scalar severity) to capture partial reversals — pooled gap and average per-stratum gap regardless of unanimity. Then StudySummary for epistemic coverage across all 3 studies.

data.Name: loop-09
data.Title: ParadoxStrength: scalar severity derived from pooled gap and per-stratum gap
data.NewConcept: ParadoxStrength on TreatmentRankings; PooledGap and AvgStratumGap on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: How large is the reversal? Can we rank studies by paradox severity, and does strength correlate with allocation imbalance?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney: pooled gap ~4.6pp favoring B, avg stratum gap ~5pp favoring A. Berkeley: larger pooled gap, varies by department. Balanced: strength=0 (no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: StudySummary: roll up ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, AvgParadoxStrength across all studies. The model now describes its own epistemic coverage in a single view.

data.Name: loop-10
data.Title: StudySummary: the model witnesses its own epistemic coverage
data.NewConcept: StudySummary table (one row per model instance): StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, UnexplainedCount, ImpossibleCount, AvgParadoxStrength
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the epistemic state? How many reversals, how many explained, how many witnessed-but-unexplained?
data.MockDataNote: 3 studies: ReversalCount=2 (kidney+berkeley), ExplainedCount=1 (kidney), UnexplainedCount=1 (berkeley), ImpossibleCount=1 (balanced). The model now holds a mirror to itself.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how heavy is each stratum? Add StratumFraction to StratumSummaries — the fraction of the study's total cases that land in each stratum. The weight each stratum carries IS the lever the confounder pulls.

data.Name: loop-11
data.Title: StratumFraction: the weight each stratum carries in the pooled average
data.NewConcept: StratumFraction (and StudyTotalCases, StratumTotalCases) on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of the study's total cases land in each stratum? High StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down — and when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, the reversal follows.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: large-stratum = (263+80)/700 = 49%; small-stratum = (87+270)/700 = 51%. Nearly equal stratum weights — but unequal per-treatment allocation (75% of A is in large; 23% of B is in large). The weight alone is not the mechanism; the differential allocation into heavy strata is.
data.NextSuggestion: Add WeightedStratumRate: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate per treatment row. Sum these across strata to reconstruct PooledRate from first principles. The DAG now witnesses the mechanism equation: pooled rate = weighted average of stratum rates.

data.Name: loop-12
data.Title: WeightedPull: mechanism equation witnessed — pooled rate = weighted sum of stratum rates
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumRate on StratumSummaries; PooledRateFromWeightsA and PooledRateFromWeightsB on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: Can the DAG reconstruct each treatment's pooled rate purely from stratum weights × stratum rates? If PooledRateFromWeights = PooledRate, the mechanism equation is verified: Simpson's Paradox is algebraically explained.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: PooledRateFromWeightsA=0.833, PooledRateFromWeightsB=0.779 (A looks better under equal study-level weights). But actual pooled rates are A=0.780, B=0.826 (B looks better). The divergence IS the mechanism: real pooled uses treatment-level allocation weights; the reconstruction uses study-level weights. The swap between the two orderings shows exactly how differential allocation flips the conclusion. balanced-synthetic: weights match exactly (equal allocation = no flip possible).
data.NextSuggestion: Add AllocationBias to StratumSummaries: the difference between TreatmentExposureFraction for A and B in each stratum. High bias toward the losing treatment in a low-success stratum IS the confounder's fingerprint as a witnessed number.

data.Name: loop-13
data.Title: AllocationBias: the confounder's fingerprint as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: AllocationBias on StratumSummaries (AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB); also AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, TreatmentACasesHere, TreatmentBCasesHere, TreatmentATotalCases, TreatmentBTotalCases
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, is one treatment disproportionately concentrated here? AllocationBias = AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB: positive means A is over-represented here, negative means B is. A strong negative bias in a low-success stratum is the confounder's fingerprint.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986 large-stratum: AllocationFractionA=263/350=0.751, AllocationFractionB=80/350=0.229, AllocationBias=+0.523. A is massively over-allocated to the hard (large-stone) stratum. This single number summarizes why the pooled rate misleads: A carries 52pp more exposure to the hard case, dragging its pooled average down below B's despite beating B in both strata.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a 4th study: kidney-balanced — same stratum rates as kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (50/50 split). AllocationBias=0 everywhere, IsReversal=FALSE. The counterfactual shows the reversal is purely an allocation artifact, not a property of the treatment rates themselves.

data.Name: loop-14
data.Title: Counterfactual: kidney-balanced isolates allocation as the sole cause of the reversal
data.NewConcept: Fourth study (kidney-balanced): same stratum rates as kidney-1986, equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationBias=0, ParadoxStrength=0.
data.DomainQuestion: If the same doctors, same disease, same treatments, same stratum difficulty — but patients were allocated equally — would the paradox still exist? The DAG answers: no. The reversal lives in the allocation, not in the treatment rates.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-balanced: PooledRateA = (163+128)/350 = 0.831, PooledRateB = (152+120)/350 = 0.777. A wins pooled AND per-stratum. IsReversal=FALSE, ParadoxStrength=0. AllocationBias=0 in both strata. The counterfactual witnesses that the reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an artifact of differential allocation, not of treatment efficacy.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ReversalThreshold to TreatmentRankings: the minimum AllocationBias magnitude needed to flip the pooled winner. How far is kidney-1986 from the threshold? How close is berkeley-1973? This quantifies paradox fragility vs robustness.

data.Name: loop-15
data.Title: ReversalThreshold: how robust or fragile is each study's paradox?
data.NewConcept: MaxAllocationBias, WeightedStratumGapSum, ThresholdMargin on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: How much allocation bias is needed to produce a reversal? Is the observed bias well above threshold (robust paradox) or barely over it (fragile paradox)? ThresholdMargin = observed bias minus minimum needed.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: ReversalIntensity=1.0, ThresholdMargin=+0.5 — maximally robust, every stratum opposes pooled. berkeley-1973: ReversalIntensity=0.75, ThresholdMargin=+0.25 — past threshold but not unanimous (3/4 departments oppose). balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced: intensity=0, margin=-0.5 — structurally impossible. ThresholdMargin > 0 means the paradox is robust; < 0 means absent or impossible.
data.NextSuggestion: Pour a real-world dataset with a known mediator (not confounder) through the same DAG and witness where it lands on the reversal_intensity × paradox_strength plane. The geometry of the plane is the next frontier.

data.Name: loop-16
data.Title: StratumGap: per-stratum rate difference as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: StratumGap field on StratumSummaries (StratumRateA - StratumRateB, signed)
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, how large is the treatment advantage, and in which direction? StratumGap > 0 means A wins here; < 0 means B wins. Summing StratumGap × StratumFraction gives the equal-weight pooled gap — a new derived node.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: small-stratum gap = +0.064 (A wins by 6.4pp), large-stratum gap = +0.043 (A wins by 4.3pp). Both positive — A wins everywhere. The pooled gap is negative (B appears to win by 4.6pp) purely due to allocation. The signed gap makes this contradiction arithmetically visible in one column. kidney-balanced: small=+0.064, large=+0.042 (same signs, same magnitudes — the rates are identical to kidney-1986 but now A also wins pooled because allocation is balanced). balanced-synthetic: small=+0.10, large=+0.10 (constant, equal allocation). berkeley-1973: dept-A=−0.200 (female wins), dept-B=+0.011 (male wins barely), dept-C=−0.002 (female wins barely), dept-D=−0.016 (female wins). Mixed signs confirm the partial paradox: no unanimous StratumGap direction, hence no clean reversal.
data.NextSuggestion: Derive WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings: SUM(StratumGap × StratumFraction) aggregated across all TreatmentA rows for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap allocation-free weighting would produce. When WeightedStratumGapSum and PooledGap have opposite signs, the allocation has not just compressed the signal but flipped it. That sign flip is a more precise characterisation of Simpson's Paradox than IsReversal: it handles partial flips and gives a magnitude to the distortion.

data.Name: loop-17
data.Title: WeightedStratumGapSum: the equal-weight signal — and the Berkeley category error witnessed
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumGap on StratumSummaries (StratumGap × StratumFraction, the per-stratum contribution to the equal-weight pooled gap); WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings (SUMIFS of WeightedStratumGap across TreatmentA rows); SignedPooledGap (PooledRateA − PooledRateB, signed); IsSignFlip boolean (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs)
data.DomainQuestion: If allocation were equal across strata, what would the pooled gap be? WeightedStratumGapSum answers this. When it has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not just compressed the signal — it has flipped it. That sign flip IS Simpson's Paradox under a continuous definition. Does Berkeley sign-flip?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: balanced-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.1000 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, no distortion). kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457 → IsSignFlip=TRUE (A wins in every stratum by 5.4pp on average, but actual pooled shows B winning by 4.6pp). kidney-balanced: WSGSUM=+0.0543, SignedPooledGap=+0.0543 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, WSGSUM = actual pooled gap). berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292 → IsSignFlip=TRUE. Per-stratum breakdown: dept-A gap=−0.2035 (female wins by 20.4pp, fraction=0.289); dept-B=−0.0496 (female wins, fraction=0.181); dept-C=+0.0286 (male wins barely, fraction=0.284); dept-D=−0.0184 (female wins, fraction=0.245). Under equal allocation female would win pooled by 6.4pp — but the actual pooled shows male winning by 12.9pp. Berkeley IS a Simpson's Paradox under the sign-flip definition despite IsReversal=FALSE. The binary unanimity criterion excluded Berkeley because dept-C goes to male; the sign-flip criterion correctly includes it.
data.NextSuggestion: The sign flip is witnessed. Now measure the magnitude: how far has the allocation bent the pooled signal? AllocationDistortion = |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Berkeley: |−0.0642 − (+0.1292)| = 0.1934. Kidney: |+0.0537 − (−0.0457)| = 0.0994. Berkeley's distortion is nearly twice kidney's — despite IsReversal=FALSE. This is the single-number measure that ranks studies by how badly confounding has distorted their pooled result.

data.Name: loop-18
data.Title: AllocationDistortion: the magnitude of the signal the allocation erased or fabricated
data.NewConcept: AllocationDistortion on TreatmentRankings (|WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|) — the scalar distance between the equal-weight signal and the actual pooled signal
data.DomainQuestion: How much has the allocation distorted the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show? AllocationDistortion=0 means the allocation is neutral. A large AllocationDistortion means the pooled number is mostly noise from case assignment, not signal about treatment efficacy.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, AllocationDistortion=0.1934. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, AllocationDistortion=0.0994. kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (balanced allocation, no distortion). balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (equal allocation, no distortion). The allocation is injecting a 19.3pp distortion into Berkeley's pooled result — reversing a real female advantage of 6.4pp into an apparent male advantage of 12.9pp. This is nearly twice the 9.9pp distortion in kidney-1986. ParadoxStrength (old measure) gives berkeley=0.0969, kidney=0.0457 — same ordinal ranking but ParadoxStrength is penalized by the unanimity criterion: Berkeley's 0.75 ReversalIntensity reduces its score. AllocationDistortion is not so penalized: it measures what the allocation DID to the signal, not whether the outcome passes an arbitrary threshold.
data.NextSuggestion: Two severity measures are now live: ParadoxStrength and AllocationDistortion. They agree on ordinal ranking across our four studies but diverge in magnitude (Berkeley is 2× kidney under AllocationDistortion but only 2.1× under ParadoxStrength). The more important question: is a study with AllocationDistortion > 0 but IsSignFlip=FALSE possible? That would be a type-C study — allocation has compressed but not reversed the signal. None of the current studies are type-C. Design a synthetic type-C study: same-sign WSGSUM and SignedPooledGap, but WSGSUM closer to zero than SignedPooledGap. Add it to the data and witness AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE.

data.Name: loop-19
data.Title: Type-C witness: compressed-synthetic — allocation distortion without sign flip
data.NewConcept: compressed-synthetic study (fifth study): type-C case — A wins every stratum and wins pooled, but allocation compresses A's pooled advantage. Witnesses AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE, IsReversal=FALSE, PerStratumWinner=A. Completes the four-type taxonomy.
data.DomainQuestion: Does a study with real allocation distortion (AllocationDistortion > 0) but no sign flip (IsSignFlip=FALSE) exist? If yes, the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy is nonempty — there is a class of misleading aggregates that compress but do not reverse the pooled signal. This class is invisible to both IsReversal and IsSignFlip but is visible to AllocationDistortion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.1934, ReversalIntensity=0.75 → type B. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.0994, ReversalIntensity=1.0 → type A. compressed-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.0600, IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0.0400, ReversalIntensity=0.0 → type C (A wins pooled by 6pp but would win by 10pp under equal allocation). kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. All four types are now empirically populated. Type-C is important: a researcher seeing pooled A=78%, B=72% and concluding 'A wins by 6pp' is correct — but the equal-weight truth is 'A wins by 10pp.' The allocation has consumed 4pp of signal without a single stratum switching winner.
data.NextSuggestion: All four DistortionType cells are now populated. Add DistortionType as a derived field on TreatmentRankings: A = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity=1.0; B = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity<1.0; C = NOT IsSignFlip AND AllocationDistortion>0.01; D = AllocationDistortion≤0.01. This makes the geometric taxonomy a first-class derived fact in the DAG. Then add PolicyImplication per type: type A = stratify-immediately; type B = investigate-confounder; type C = check-allocation-bias; type D = pooled-analysis-trustworthy.

data.Name: loop-20
data.Title: Hydrator regression: write kidney-1986 through the adapter and diff against known-correct output
data.NewConcept: Implementation-layer infrastructure, not a new domain concept: DevDbHydrator (idempotent upsert of rulebook leaves into dev Postgres), one boring explicit adapter for kidney-1986, round-trip diff. The rulebook remains the analytic contract; this loop proves the hydrator works before any new data enters.
data.DomainQuestion: Can kidney-1986 — whose CaseCells are already in the rulebook — be written through a hydrator adapter, upserted into the dev DB, materialized by the existing Postgres inference chain, and produce AllocationDistortion/IsSignFlip/DistortionType numbers that exactly match the witnessed values from loop-19?
data.MockDataNote: Steps: (1) Store source capture under data/raw/kidney-1986/ with provenance note (Charig et al. BMJ 1986, table extracted manually). (2) Write scripts/adapters/kidney_1986.py (or .ts) that returns the five canonical leaf tables: Studies, Treatments, Strata, StratumVariables, CaseCells — boring and explicit, no abstraction. (3) Write scripts/hydrate.py that idempotently upserts adapter output into the dev DB, tagged by study_id and ingestion_run_id. (4) Run the existing Postgres view chain. (5) Diff computed output against expected values: PooledRateA=0.780, PooledRateB=0.826, IsReversal=TRUE, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.0994, DistortionType=A. If all match, the hydrator is validated. Success criterion is a passing diff, not a visualizer change.
data.NextSuggestion: Hydrator validated. Now pour one new non-canonical real study through the same adapter contract (loop-20b). The new study should be consequential, have a clean 2×N shape, and not be a known Simpson example — so the instrument makes a genuine claim rather than reproducing a textbook result.

data.Name: loop-20b
data.Title: First new real study: pour a non-canonical published dataset through the validated hydrator
data.NewConcept: One new real study row set, produced by an explicit adapter and entered through the loop-20 hydrator. The domain concept is provenance: source URL/PDF/table/page, extraction date, and normalization notes stored under data/raw/<study_id>/.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the instrument produce a geometrically informative result (a non-trivial DistortionType and AllocationDistortion value) when a real published study that is NOT a known Simpson example is poured through the hydrator?
data.MockDataNote: Candidate criteria: two comparison groups, discrete strata, successes and total cases available per cell, denominators unambiguous, adapter writable in under an hour. Good candidate domains: clinical outcomes by risk group, sentencing outcomes by legally meaningful strata, education outcomes by program/school type, hospital/surgeon volume outcomes. The result is informative regardless of DistortionType — a type-D (neutral) result is a valid finding, not a failure. Do not generalize the adapter until this study survives the round trip.
data.NextSuggestion: After two real studies have survived the round trip, add schema fields the real import proves are needed: provenance, completeness flags, RawDataRequired. Do not generalize the hydrator before witnessing what breaks.

data.Name: loop-21
data.Title: DistortionType + PolicyImplication: the four-type taxonomy as a first-class DAG fact
data.NewConcept: DistortionType (derived field on TreatmentRankings): A = full sign-flip unanimous reversal; B = sign-flip non-unanimous; C = distortion without sign flip; D = neutral. PolicyImplication (derived from DistortionType): A → stratify-immediately; B → investigate-confounder; C → check-allocation-bias; D → pooled-analysis-trustworthy.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the geometric taxonomy (four cells defined by IsSignFlip × ReversalIntensity × AllocationDistortion magnitude) be collapsed into a single derived field that a researcher or policy layer can read without knowing the internal coordinates?
data.MockDataNote: All five current studies resolve cleanly: kidney-1986 → type A (stratify-immediately); berkeley-1973 → type B (investigate-confounder); compressed-synthetic → type C (check-allocation-bias); kidney-balanced → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy); balanced-synthetic → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy). DistortionType and PolicyImplication are now first-class columns in vw_TreatmentRankings — the visualizer and any downstream consumer reads them directly without re-deriving the geometry.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts ReversalCount and NonReversalCount using the old IsReversal (unanimity) criterion. Add DistortionType aggregations: TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount (TypeA+TypeB), and AvgAllocationDistortion. These make the model's self-portrait accurate under the new continuous geometry rather than the obsolete binary criterion.

data.Name: loop-22
data.Title: Third real dataset: a new domain poured through the hydrator
data.NewConcept: ThirdRealStudy row set produced by an adapter, not hand-authored directly in the rulebook. The new study enters through the same dev DB hydration script used in loop-20.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the instrument's geometry generalize beyond the two initial smoke-test studies when the data arrives through the adapter/hydrator path?
data.MockDataNote: Choose a consequential domain with a clean 2×N shape: clinical outcomes by risk group, sentencing by legally meaningful strata, education outcomes by program/school type, or another published setting with successes and total cases by group and stratum. Priority criteria: source tables are inspectable, denominators are unambiguous, and the adapter can be written in under an hour. The result is informative whether it lands as sign-flip, distortion-no-flip, or neutral.
data.NextSuggestion: Once three real datasets have round-tripped, compare geometric distortion to the annotated causal role. This tests whether AllocationDistortion is measuring causal confounding, allocation-induced distortion, or both.

data.Name: loop-23
data.Title: CausalStructureSignal: compare distortion geometry to causal role after real ingestion
data.NewConcept: CausalRoleGroup field on StratumVariables; AvgDistortionByRole aggregation on ModelSummary; optional RealDataOnly filter so synthetic controls do not contaminate empirical summaries.
data.DomainQuestion: After real studies enter through the hydrator, do true confounders, contested variables, mediators, and neutral strata produce different AllocationDistortion patterns?
data.MockDataNote: Current seed data suggests that a contested causal role can still produce very high arithmetic distortion. The empirical question should therefore be framed carefully: AllocationDistortion may not identify a causal confounder; it identifies allocation-induced signal distortion. CausalRole explains why the distortion exists; the geometry measures how much it bent the observed aggregate.
data.NextSuggestion: Make the geometric classification first-class so the visualizer and policy layers do not need to re-derive it ad hoc.

data.Name: loop-24
data.Title: DistortionType: classify studies by geometry independent of causal label
data.NewConcept: DistortionType field on TreatmentRankings derived from IsSignFlip, ReversalIntensity, and AllocationDistortion magnitude; optional DistortionTypeLabel and DistortionTypeDescription for the visualizer.
data.DomainQuestion: Can every hydrated study be classified into a small number of geometrically meaningful outcomes without relying on a causal story?
data.MockDataNote: Proposed types: A = full-reversal (IsSignFlip=true and ReversalIntensity=1.0); B = partial-reversal/sign-flip (IsSignFlip=true and ReversalIntensity<1.0); C = distortion-no-flip (IsSignFlip=false and AllocationDistortion above threshold); D = neutral (AllocationDistortion near zero). Loop-20 may implement this temporarily in the UI for color-coding, but this loop makes it a rulebook-derived fact.
data.NextSuggestion: Now deliberately seek a real type-C case: measurable distortion without a sign flip. This tests whether type-C is merely synthetic or common in published work.

data.Name: loop-25
data.Title: Type-C real-data witness: allocation distortion without sign flip
data.NewConcept: A real, published study that hydrates as DistortionType=C if AllocationDistortion>threshold and IsSignFlip=false.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the published literature contain studies where the pooled winner is directionally correct but the margin is materially compressed or inflated by allocation?
data.MockDataNote: Candidate pattern: senior surgeons, hospitals, treatments, schools, or programs receive systematically harder cases, but the superior group still wins pooled. The adapter should prove that the pooled conclusion is not wrong, but its magnitude is wrong. This is practically important because many policy decisions depend on effect size, not just winner identity.
data.NextSuggestion: Map each DistortionType to an epistemic action so the visualizer can tell a researcher what to do next.

data.Name: loop-26
data.Title: PolicyImplication: convert geometry into researcher action
data.NewConcept: PolicyImplication field on TreatmentRankings derived from DistortionType, IsParadoxExplained, InferenceReadiness, and RawDataRequired.
data.DomainQuestion: Given a study's geometry and data-readiness state, what should a researcher do with the pooled conclusion?
data.MockDataNote: Type A: stratify immediately; the pooled conclusion is directionally wrong. Type B: investigate the stratifying mechanism; the aggregate has sign-flipped but the per-stratum story is mixed. Type C: report adjusted or standardized margins; the winner may be right but the effect size is distorted. Type D: pooled analysis is likely safe with respect to this stratification. If RawDataRequired=true or InferenceReadiness fails, the implication is 'insufficient data' rather than a substantive judgment.
data.NextSuggestion: Test those policy implications retrospectively against historical cases where pooled analysis contributed to a wrong or fragile decision.

data.Name: loop-27
data.Title: Retrospective validation: would the instrument have flagged known historical errors?
data.NewConcept: RetrospectiveTestStudy rows hydrated through the same adapter path; PlaneCoordinateAtDecisionTime vs PlaneCoordinateWithFullData; optional HistoricalDecisionOutcome annotation.
data.DomainQuestion: Given only the data that was available at the time of a consequential decision, would the instrument have recommended stratify-immediately, investigate-confounder, or insufficient-data?
data.MockDataNote: Use the loop-20 hydrator, not a bespoke notebook. Candidate cases should have a clear historical pooled claim and later evidence that stratification or confounding mattered. The key validation is whether the rulebook could have raised the warning before the error was widely recognized.
data.NextSuggestion: If retrospective validation works, shift from hand-picked studies to a small literature screen using the ScreeningApproximation and adapter contract.

data.Name: loop-28
data.Title: Literature screen: rank candidate studies using published tables
data.NewConcept: LiteratureCandidates table or external candidate manifest; ScreeningApprox coordinates; RawDataRequired flag; CandidatePriorityScore.
data.DomainQuestion: Which currently published studies appear to have high allocation-induced distortion from their published summaries alone?
data.MockDataNote: This loop should not scrape the visualizer. It should scrape or manually capture source papers/tables into the adapter input format, then produce candidate coordinates. The visualizer reads the exported candidate rulebook or live DB view. Candidate domains: clinical subgroup tables, education outcome tables, hospital/surgeon volume studies, sentencing/outcome tables, or other settings where group allocation across strata is visibly non-random.
data.NextSuggestion: Pick the highest-priority candidate and obtain enough source detail to compute exact coordinates, then compare exact DistortionType to the screening classification.

data.Name: loop-29
data.Title: Prospective validation: confirm a high-scoring literature candidate
data.NewConcept: ProspectiveValidationStudy; ExactVsScreeningClassification; ProspectiveClassificationAccuracy on ModelSummary.
data.DomainQuestion: Does a candidate flagged by the screening process retain the same DistortionType and high AllocationDistortion when exact or near-exact CaseCells are hydrated?
data.MockDataNote: The success criterion is not that every candidate is paradoxical. The success criterion is that the screening approximation points the hydrator toward studies where the exact geometry is worth inspecting. Confirming a type-B or type-C currently influential study would be the strongest prospective result.
data.NextSuggestion: After prospective validation, formalize the instrument so another researcher can apply it without knowing the internal DAG.

data.Name: loop-30
data.Title: Instrument specification: adapter contract, five fields, four types, and screening formula
data.NewConcept: InstrumentSpec table: one row per required input, derived coordinate, classification rule, adapter contract requirement, and screening approximation formula.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the full instrument be specified precisely enough that another researcher or script can hydrate a new study, compute the geometry, and understand the classification?
data.MockDataNote: The spec should include both the analytic coordinates and the implementation contract proven in loop-20: required leaf tables, acceptable data modes, provenance fields, validation checks, SignedPooledGap, WeightedStratumGapSum, IsSignFlip, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType, PolicyImplication, and ScreeningApproximation. Every claim should be machine-verifiable against the current seed plus real hydrated datasets.
data.NextSuggestion: Formally retire or demote the old unanimity-based IsReversal in favor of IsReversal_v2=IsSignFlip, while preserving the old field as a stricter subtype.

data.Name: loop-31
data.Title: IsReversal_v2: retire the unanimity criterion as the primary definition
data.NewConcept: IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip; DefinitionDelta = IsReversal_v2 XOR IsReversal; StrictReversalSubtype preserving the old unanimity criterion.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the sign-flip definition strictly generalize the old full-unanimity definition across synthetic controls, canonical examples, and newly hydrated real datasets?
data.MockDataNote: Expected pattern: old full reversals remain positive under IsReversal_v2; partial sign-flips such as Berkeley become positive; type-C and type-D remain negative for directional reversal but still carry AllocationDistortion if applicable. The conclusion becomes cleaner: IsReversal is a strict subtype, IsSignFlip is the primary Simpson-type reversal criterion, and AllocationDistortion is the continuous severity measure.
data.NextSuggestion: Write the preprint abstract or technical memo from the witnessed coordinates, the adapter-backed real-data validation, and the rulebook/Postgres/visualizer round trip.

data.Name: loop-32
data.Title: CorrectionCost: how hard is this paradox to detect and correct?
data.NewConcept: CorrectionCost on TreatmentRankings: estimated minimum sample size per stratum needed to detect the reversal with 80% power; DetectionDifficulty scalar
data.DomainQuestion: Some paradoxes are easy to detect (large strata, obvious allocation imbalance) and some are nearly invisible (small strata, subtle bias). CorrectionCost estimates how large a study would need to be to reliably surface this reversal. High cost + high severity = high danger.
data.MockDataNote: Synthetic: a paradox with AllocationBias=0.05 (subtle) and StratumGap=0.02 (small effect) requires very large samples to detect. CorrectionCost=HIGH. A paradox with AllocationBias=0.52 (kidney-1986) is detectable with modest samples. CorrectionCost=LOW. The plane now has a cost contour.
data.NextSuggestion: Combine PolicyReversalSeverity and CorrectionCost into a DangerScore: severity / correction_cost. High severity that is cheap to detect is less dangerous than moderate severity that requires enormous samples. DangerScore ranks studies by practical risk.

data.Name: loop-33
data.Title: DangerScore: severity / correction_cost — which paradoxes are most dangerous in practice?
data.NewConcept: DangerScore on TreatmentRankings (PolicyReversalSeverity / CorrectionCost); DangerRank across all studies in the model
data.DomainQuestion: Which studies in the model pose the greatest practical risk? DangerScore ranks them: a study with high severity and low correction cost should be fixed immediately; one with low severity and high correction cost may be acceptable to leave unresolved.
data.MockDataNote: Hypothetical ranking: PublicationBias synthetic (high severity, very high correction cost because unpublished studies are hard to find) scores highest. kidney-1986 (moderate severity, low correction cost — just stratify on stone size) scores lower. DangerScore operationalizes the plane as a triage tool.
data.NextSuggestion: Survey the published epidemiology and clinical trial literature for studies that have high reversal_intensity and high paradox_strength but have not been stratified. These are the undetected paradoxes. The DAG is now a screening instrument.

data.Name: loop-34
data.Title: Literature screen: which published studies are high-risk candidates for undetected Simpson's Paradox?
data.NewConcept: LiteratureCandidate studies sourced from published research with known allocation imbalance and unstratified pooled analysis; preliminary plane coordinates computed from published summary statistics
data.DomainQuestion: Can the plane coordinates (reversal_intensity × paradox_strength) be approximated from published summary statistics alone, without raw data? If yes, the DAG becomes a screening tool applicable to any published pooled analysis.
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after literature survey. Candidates: cholesterol-lowering trials with age-stratified subgroups, COVID-19 vaccine trials with comorbidity strata, surgical outcome studies with severity stratification. Each gets preliminary plane coordinates from published summary tables.
data.NextSuggestion: Select the highest-scoring literature candidate and request the raw data (or simulate from published summary statistics) to compute exact plane coordinates. This is the first external validation of the DAG as a screening instrument.

data.Name: loop-35
data.Title: External validation: compute exact plane coordinates for a high-risk literature candidate
data.NewConcept: First external validation study poured through the full DAG pipeline; comparison of screening estimate (from published summaries) vs exact coordinates (from raw data)
data.DomainQuestion: How close are the screening-estimated plane coordinates to the exact coordinates computed from raw data? If they agree within 10%, the summary-statistic screening method is validated. If they diverge, what additional information is needed?
data.MockDataNote: To be filled after raw data is obtained. The key metric: does the screening correctly classify the study as above or below threshold_margin=0? Classification accuracy is the primary validation criterion.
data.NextSuggestion: Repeat external validation on 3-5 more literature candidates, building a validation set. Once the screening method is validated, publish the plane, the screening formula, and the instrument description.

data.Name: loop-36
data.Title: Validation set: 3-5 external studies confirm screening accuracy across diverse domains
data.NewConcept: Validation set of 3-5 external studies; ScreeningAccuracy metric; domain coverage (medicine, economics, social science, education)
data.DomainQuestion: Does the screening method correctly classify above/below threshold across diverse domains and sample sizes? A validated multi-domain screening instrument is the primary deliverable of this entire project.
data.MockDataNote: Target domains: (1) a medical RCT with severity stratification, (2) an economics study with income stratification, (3) an education study with socioeconomic stratification, (4) a social science study with demographic stratification. Cross-domain validation establishes the instrument's generality.
data.NextSuggestion: Write up the instrument: the plane definition, the screening formula, the threshold_margin=0 boundary, the DangerScore ranking, and the validation results. This is the publishable finding.

data.Name: loop-37
data.Title: Instrument definition: the plane, the boundary, the screening formula, and the danger score
data.NewConcept: Formal instrument specification derived from the DAG: (1) plane axes definition, (2) threshold_margin=0 boundary formula, (3) summary-statistic screening approximation, (4) DangerScore ranking formula, (5) validation criteria
data.DomainQuestion: Can the full instrument be specified precisely enough that another researcher could apply it to a new dataset without access to the DAG? The instrument specification is the distillation of all 37 loops into a usable tool.
data.MockDataNote: Instrument components: Axis 1 = StrataWonByLoser/StratumCount. Axis 2 = |PooledGap| × Axis1. Boundary = Axis1 > 0.5. Screening formula = approximable from published subgroup summaries. DangerScore = PolicyReversalSeverity/CorrectionCost. Each component traceable to a specific DAG node.
data.NextSuggestion: Submit instrument for peer review or post as a preprint. The DAG and the rulebook are the supplementary materials — the full derivation chain from raw case counts to instrument specification is machine-verifiable.

data.Name: loop-38
data.Title: Preprint: the DAG-derived Simpson's Paradox screening instrument
data.NewConcept: Preprint manuscript with DAG as supplementary material; reviewable derivation chain from CaseCell raw inputs to DangerScore output
data.DomainQuestion: Is the instrument's derivation chain convincing to a methodologically sophisticated reader who has not seen the DAG? The preprint tests whether the DAG's outputs can be communicated without the DAG itself being understood.
data.MockDataNote: Preprint sections: (1) Problem statement — binary IsReversal misclassifies Berkeley-type studies. (2) The plane — two continuous axes derived from case-cell data. (3) The threshold — threshold_margin=0 is the precise boundary. (4) Validation — N studies across M domains. (5) Screening formula — applicable to published summaries.
data.NextSuggestion: After preprint feedback, identify the weakest assumption in the instrument (most likely: the StratumFraction weight uses study-level denominators rather than treatment-level denominators for the threshold formula). Resolve it in a loop that introduces treatment-level stratum weights.

data.Name: loop-39
data.Title: TreatmentLevelStratumWeight: correct the threshold formula for non-symmetric allocation
data.NewConcept: TreatmentLevelStratumWeight on StratumSummaries (StratumCases_T / TotalCases_T per treatment, not per study); RevisedThresholdMargin using treatment-level weights
data.DomainQuestion: The current threshold_margin uses study-level stratum fractions. But the pooled rate uses treatment-level weights. Is the threshold formula off by this discrepancy? The loop computes both and witnesses whether they give materially different threshold classifications.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: study-level small-stratum weight = 0.51; treatment-A small-stratum weight = 0.249; treatment-B small-stratum weight = 0.771. Large discrepancy. RevisedThresholdMargin should be computed from treatment-level weights to match the actual pooled rate formula. If it changes the classification of any study, the current threshold is wrong.
data.NextSuggestion: With the correct threshold formula, recompute plane coordinates for all studies in the model. If any study changes quadrant (above/below threshold), the corrected formula is the publishable refinement. If none change, the study-level approximation is confirmed as sufficient.

data.Name: loop-40
data.Title: Recomputed plane: do corrected treatment-level weights change any study's quadrant?
data.NewConcept: Revised plane coordinates for all studies using TreatmentLevelStratumWeight; QuadrantChange boolean per study
data.DomainQuestion: Under the corrected threshold formula, does any study change from above-threshold to below-threshold or vice versa? A quadrant change means the study-level approximation was not just imprecise but qualitatively wrong. No quadrant change confirms the approximation is sufficient for screening.
data.MockDataNote: To be computed after loop-39. Hypothesis: no quadrant changes for the current four studies (kidney-1986 is so far above threshold that the approximation error doesn't matter). Berkeley-1973 is the risk case — it's closest to the boundary at 0.25 margin.
data.NextSuggestion: Close the loop: return to the original question (is the binary IsReversal definition correct?) with all evidence assembled. Propose a revised definition: IsReversal_v2 = threshold_margin > 0, replacing the unanimity criterion. Show that IsReversal_v2 correctly classifies Berkeley as a reversal.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic
data.Title: Balanced Synthetic Study (degenerate case — reversal structurally impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that perfectly balanced treatment allocation across strata algebraically prevents Simpson's Paradox. Not based on real data.

data.Name: berkeley-1973
data.Title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions (Bickel et al. 1975) — contested confounder
data.Source: Bickel PJ, Hammel EA, O'Connell JW. Sex Bias in Graduate Admissions: Data from Berkeley. Science. 1975;187:398-404.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic
data.Title: Compressed Synthetic Study (type-C — allocation distortion without sign flip)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that allocation can distort the pooled signal (AllocationDistortion > 0) without reversing it (IsSignFlip=FALSE). Treatment A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled — but the pooled margin is compressed by differential allocation. This populates the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy.

data.Name: kidney-1986
data.Title: Kidney Stone Treatment Study (Charig et al. 1986)
data.Source: Charig CR et al. Comparison of treatment of renal calculi by open surgery, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy. Br Med J. 1986;292:879.

data.Name: kidney-balanced
data.Title: Kidney Stone Counterfactual (balanced allocation — reversal algebraically impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset derived from Charig 1986 kidney stone rates but with equal treatment allocation across strata (175 cases per cell). Demonstrates that the reversal in the original study is purely an allocation artifact.

data.Name: radelet-1981
data.Title: Racial Characteristics and the Death Penalty in Florida (Radelet 1981)
data.Source: Radelet ML. Racial characteristics and the imposition of the death penalty. American Sociological Review. 1981;46(6):918-927.

data.Name: reintjes-2000
data.Title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis and UTI Rates in Dutch Hospitals (Reintjes et al. 2000)
data.Source: Reintjes R, de Boer A, van Pelt W, Mintjes-de Groot J. Simpson's paradox: an example from hospital epidemiology. Epidemiology. 2000;11(1):81-83. PMID: 10615849.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-A
data.Description: Synthetic treatment A — 60% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-B
data.Description: Synthetic treatment B — 50% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-female
data.Description: Female applicants (labeled B). Pooled admission rate appears lower, but per-department rates are equal or higher in most departments.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-male
data.Description: Male applicants (labeled A). Pooled admission rate appears higher than female.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-A
data.Description: Treatment A — 70% success rate in hard stratum, 90% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the hard stratum (60% of A cases go to hard). A wins in both strata and also wins pooled, but pooled margin is compressed by the allocation imbalance.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-B
data.Description: Treatment B — 60% success rate in hard stratum, 80% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the easy stratum (60% of B cases go to easy). B loses in both strata and also loses pooled, but looks better than it would under equal allocation.

data.Name: kidney-1986-A
data.Description: Open surgery / percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) — the more invasive procedure, used preferentially for large stones.

data.Name: kidney-1986-B
data.Description: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) — the less invasive procedure, used more for small stones.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-A
data.Description: Open surgery / PCNL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:93.1%, large:73.0%) but now allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment A.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-B
data.Description: ESWL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:86.7%, large:68.8%) but allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment B.

data.Name: radelet-1981-A
data.Description: White defendant. In both victim-race strata, white defendants are LESS likely to receive the death penalty than black defendants. Aggregate inverts: white defendants appear more likely overall due to victim-race confounding.

data.Name: radelet-1981-B
data.Description: Black defendant. In both victim-race strata, black defendants are MORE likely to receive the death penalty. Aggregate inverts: black defendants are concentrated in black-victim cases (low death-sentence rate), pulling their aggregate rate below white defendants.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-A
data.Description: Antibiotic prophylaxis given. Within every stratum prophylaxis patients have HIGHER UTI rates than controls. Aggregate inverts due to confounding by hospital incidence level.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-B
data.Description: No antibiotic prophylaxis (control). Within every stratum controls have LOWER UTI rates. Aggregate inverts: controls appear to have higher rates pooled.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-easy
data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-hard
data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-A
data.Description: Dept A — highly competitive (low admission rate ~64%). Women applied here at roughly equal rates to men.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-B
data.Description: Dept B — competitive (~35% admission). Women applied here less often.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-C
data.Description: Dept C — moderately selective (~34% admission). More balanced gender application.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-D
data.Description: Dept D — open (~64% admission). Women applied here most heavily — this is the easy department that women disproportionately chose.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-easy
data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rates (A=90%, B=80%). Treatment B is over-allocated here (60% of B cases vs 40% of A cases). This partially offsets B's per-stratum disadvantage in the pooled analysis.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-hard
data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rates (A=70%, B=60%). Treatment A is over-allocated here (60% of A cases vs 40% of B cases). This is the stratum that pulls A's pooled rate down relative to equal-weight expectation.

data.Name: kidney-1986-large
data.Description: Patients with large kidney stones — harder to treat, lower baseline success rate regardless of treatment. Preferentially assigned to Treatment A.

data.Name: kidney-1986-small
data.Description: Patients with small kidney stones — easier to treat, higher baseline success rate regardless of treatment.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-large
data.Description: Large kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 large stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-small
data.Description: Small kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 small stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Counterfactual stratum.

data.Name: radelet-1981-black-victim
data.Description: Cases with a black victim. Death penalty rare here (~3.3% pooled). White def: 0/9=0.00%. Black def: 6/103=5.83%. Black defendants STILL more likely. Most black defendants are in this low-rate stratum (103/166=62%), dragging their aggregate rate down.

data.Name: radelet-1981-white-victim
data.Description: Cases with a white victim. Death penalty issued far more often here (~17% pooled). White def: 19/151=12.58%. Black def: 11/63=17.46%. Black defendants MORE likely within this stratum.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-high
data.Description: High-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate >2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 22/166=13.25%. Control rate: 99/1520=6.51%. Prophylaxis is again harmful. Controls are heavily concentrated here (1520 of 2240=68%), inflating the aggregate control rate.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-low
data.Description: Low-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate ≤2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 20/1113=1.80%. Control rate: 5/720=0.69%. Prophylaxis is harmful here. Most prophylaxis patients land in this stratum (87%), pulling the aggregate prophylaxis rate down and fabricating an apparent protective effect pooled.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-stratum
data.MechanismNote: The stratum variable affects outcome (easy vs hard conditions) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — each treatment receives exactly 50% of its cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is independent of stratum, no confounding can occur and the pooled rate must agree with every per-stratum comparison.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-department
data.MechanismNote: Department choice correlates with both gender (women applied disproportionately to competitive departments) and admission rate (competitive departments admit fewer applicants regardless of gender). However, department choice may itself be downstream of gender — if women systematically self-select into harder departments due to societal factors, then department is a mediator, not a confounder. The causal role is genuinely contested in the literature. IsConfounder=FALSE because CausalRole='contested', not because the flags are false.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-stratum
data.MechanismNote: Case difficulty affects both which treatment patients receive (A is over-allocated to hard cases: 60% vs 40%) and the outcome (hard cases have lower success rates for both treatments). This IS confounding — but the confounding is not strong enough to flip the pooled winner. A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled. The allocation compresses A's pooled advantage (from equal-weight 10pp down to actual 6pp) but does not erase it. This is type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.

data.Name: kidney-1986-stone-size
data.MechanismNote: Large kidney stones were preferentially assigned to Treatment A (the more aggressive procedure), while small stones more often received Treatment B. Independently, large stones have lower baseline cure rates regardless of treatment. This dual influence — on both assignment and on outcome — is what drives the pooled reversal.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-stone-size
data.MechanismNote: In this counterfactual, stone size still affects outcome (hard cases have lower success rates) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — both treatments receive exactly 50% of their cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is now independent of stone size, no confounding occurs and AllocationBias=0 everywhere. IsConfounder=FALSE (AffectsTreatmentAssignment=false). The reversal is impossible by construction.

data.Name: radelet-1981-victim-race
data.MechanismNote: Victim race affects both defendant race (cross-race homicides are rare — most black defendants killed black victims, most white defendants killed white victims) and sentencing outcome (white-victim cases receive the death penalty ~5× more often: ~17.7% vs ~3.3%). This is classic confounding. Black defendants are disproportionately concentrated in the black-victim stratum (low sentence rate), pulling their aggregate death penalty rate below white defendants' rate — even though black defendants are MORE likely to be sentenced to death within each victim-race stratum.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-hospital-incidence
data.MechanismNote: Hospital baseline UTI incidence affects both whether a patient received prophylaxis (high-incidence hospitals were more likely to forgo it, concentrating controls there) and the outcome (high-incidence hospitals have higher UTI rates regardless of treatment). Most prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals (1113/1279=87%), which depresses the aggregate prophylaxis UTI rate below the aggregate control rate — inverting the within-stratum finding.
